questions.scenario:
A receipt worker starts sending duplicate emails. Evidence:

worker release: 2026-05-02.9
idempotency log: missing key for orderId ord_1042
queue visible messages: 3,200
checkout API: healthy
customer impact: duplicate receipts, orders still created correctly

Which control best buys time?

options.text: Pause or reduce the receipt worker desired count while preserving queued messages and preparing a fix or rollback.
Disable the checkout API immediately because the worker bug proves orders are not being created.
Increase worker desired count so duplicates finish faster.
Delete all queue messages because duplicate emails are less visible if no work remains.

questions.explanation: The damage is in the worker side effect, not the checkout API. Pausing or reducing consumers can stop more duplicate emails while keeping the queue as the work record. It is smaller and easier to reverse than disabling all checkout.

questions.scenario:
A vendor API is rate-limiting DevPolaris:

provider response: 429 Too Many Requests
worker desired=10 running=10
errors per minute: rising with worker count
queue age: rising slowly
orders API: healthy

What control matches the failure?

options.text: Reduce worker desired count or cap concurrency so the system stops overwhelming the provider.
Increase API desired count because worker errors come from too few web tasks.
Start a new ECS rollout for the API because vendor rate limits are target health failures.
Disable DLQ handling so retries never become visible.

questions.explanation: The provider is the saturated layer. More workers create more failing calls, so a limit buys time. The queue may grow, but that is often safer than hammering a dependency that is already rejecting traffic.

questions.scenario:
A scheduled cleanup job is deleting active export files:

schedule: every 15 minutes
last run: deleted 312 files with prefix prod/current/
expected prefix: prod/tmp/
next run: in 7 minutes

What is the safest first control?

options.text: Disable the schedule or stop the job trigger before the next run, then investigate and fix the prefix bug.
Increase API desired count because more web tasks can recreate deleted files faster.
Scale receipt workers to zero because every operational incident starts with workers.
Let one more run happen so the team can collect more deleted examples.

questions.explanation: The active risk is the next scheduled run. Disabling the schedule is a small reversible control that stops more deletion while the team investigates. A code fix still matters, but the control buys time.

questions.scenario:
An operational-control table for the orders runtime includes:

increase API desired count
lower API desired count
pause worker desired count
disable schedule
reserve Lambda concurrency
stop ECS service deployment with rollback

Which are valid examples of controls that can buy time?

options.text: Changing API desired count when API capacity or dependency pressure is the issue.
Pausing or reducing workers when side work is unsafe or a dependency is overloaded.
Disabling a risky schedule or stopping an unsafe rollout with rollback.
Editing production code directly inside a running container so no release record is needed.

questions.explanation: Operational controls change runtime behavior to contain damage. They should be deliberate, reversible, and matched to the failure. Editing a running container bypasses the release record and is not the habit this article is teaching.

questions.scenario:
A control is proposed:

action: pause receipt workers for 30 minutes
benefit: stop duplicate emails from bad release 2026-05-02.9
cost: receipt queue age will rise and some customers receive emails late
rollback: set worker desired count back to 4 after fixed revision passes idempotency check

What should the on-call do with this tradeoff?

options.text: Record the reason, expected side effect, owner, and reversal condition before applying the control.
Apply it silently because controls are not user-visible changes.
Reject it because every control with a downside is worse than doing nothing.
Delete queue metrics so delayed email does not appear on dashboards.

questions.explanation: Controls buy time, but they still have consequences. A good control note says why it is being used, what pain it creates, who owns it, and when to reverse it. That makes containment safer and easier to hand off.
